input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A cyber‑physical twin of a building that integrates static Building Information Models (BIM) with runtime data from sensors, actuators, and controllers.  The physical asset includes architectural, structural, and mechanical subsystems and is used in the architecture, engineering, construction, and operations (AECO) domain throughout the entire building lifecycle—from initial design through operational monitoring and refurbishment.  This combination of detailed static models with real‑time data makes it amenable to digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers integrated services that include: • Real‑time monitoring of all BCS components through continuous data exchange with the physical building; • Predictive analytics that forecast equipment wear, energy consumption and fault likelihood; • Optimization services that suggest control set‑point adjustments to reduce energy use and improve occupant comfort; • Direct control over actuators and HVAC units based on user‑defined logic; • Interactive, immersive visualization interfaces that allow facility managers to inspect building conditions and control operations from a unified dashboard.  Primary users are Building Control Engineers who model devices and configure logic, Facility Managers who monitor and command building operations, and DT Engineers who develop and deploy extensions or analytical plugins.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The solution is implemented through a Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) framework that extends Building Information Modeling (BIM) to capture full BCS definitions (sensors, actuators, controllers).  BIM models are transformed into structured runtime representations via automated code generation and deployment artifacts with minimal manual intervention.  Custom services and analytics are integrated using standardized extension points and plugin interfaces, enabling DT Engineers to add new capabilities without modifying core architecture.  The framework supports real‑time data exchange layers, interactive visualization components, and continuous synchronization between the physical building and its Digital Twin.  The entire approach is supported by research projects TwinLight and BOREALIS, which provide domain‑specific guidelines and best‑practice artefacts for deployment across complex, heterogeneous infrastructure environments.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin framework supports continuous real‑time data exchange, ensuring the digital representation stays synchronised with its physical counterpart in real time. The system provides bidirectional communication that enables up‑to‑date monitoring and control without explicit fixed sampling intervals in the document, indicating a design aimed at real‑time interaction with minimal latency.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin constellations are orchestrated through a modular, service‑oriented architecture centered on a DT Framework that serves as a central hub. The framework offers standardized extension points enabling the integration of custom services and analytics, while runtime configuration management allows adaptations without full redeployment. Component integration follows a service‑oriented pattern: Building Control Engineers use BIM tools to model devices and trigger deployment, DT Engineers extend the framework by plugging in specialized analytics or control services, and Facility Managers interact with the deployed DT for real‑time monitoring and control. Resource management is supported by a scalable broker within the framework, allowing scaling from component‑level to entire infrastructure systems. Maintainability is achieved via low‑overhead updates, and interoperability across heterogeneous systems is ensured through common data models and runtime agreements. Overall orchestration guarantees bidirectional continuous data exchange between the physical building and its digital twin, facilitating a holistic approach to building life‑cycle management.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system integrates with external environments via a modular framework that exposes standardized extension points for service integration and supports bidirectional data exchange. While specific protocols are not detailed, the design emphasizes interoperability across heterogeneous systems, allowing the framework to serve as a bridge for external services, analytics, and enterprise systems within the AECO domain.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The digital twin is constructed from a Building Information Modeling (BIM) artefact enriched with device configurations (sensors, actuators, controllers). These models are processed by a Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) pipeline that extracts structured, typed representations and automatically generates deployable twin artefacts. The artefacts contain geometric data from BIM for immersive visualization, behavioural logic encoded in control‑logic blocks (e.g., lighting control workflows), and analytical metadata such as device type and location. Runtime data flows are handled by Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto, which synchronize physical sensor readings and actuator states with the twin in real‑time. The framework’s extension points allow additional analytics or custom DT services to be integrated.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is implemented by interpreting BIM‑defined lighting fixtures as LightTwinComponents that inject UE5 light objects into the scene. Commands are transmitted via the UnrealTwinController, which observes DittoDevice state changes and publishes updates through DittoConnection endpoints, enabling remote control of the physical LED driver. Safety constraints and actuation limits are enforced by Ditto’s policy‑based validation, which ensures on/off, brightness, and colour‑temperature commands stay within defined units and actuator capability ranges. The system supports bidirectional actuation: physical device state changes generate Ditto events that are reflected back in the UE5 scene.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing metadata is embedded in the BIM model under device tags that specify sensor types; in the presented case, photometric sensors provide illumination, colour‑temperature, and on/off status for each fixture. These sensors publish measurements to Eclipse Hono, which routes them to DittoDevices representing each sensor. Ditto validates units and numerical ranges before storage. TwinComponent pipelines read these events through FeatureParserControllers (e.g., LightFeatureParser) to update the UE5 rendering. Sampling frequency and bandwidth are governed by the physical device firmware, but are synchronized to the DT via Ditto’s throttling policies and continuous event streams.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured by validating the enriched BIM model against a custom schema before pipeline execution, guaranteeing that all required semantic annotations are present. Runtime fidelity is preserved through Ditto’s policy‑based validation, which enforces value ranges, units, and pre‑execution safety checks on actuator commands. Fault tolerance is achieved with Eclipse Hono’s at‑least‑once message delivery guaranteeing message receipt during network interruptions, and Ditto’s persistent event store that preserves state even during service disruptions. Quality assurance is performed via continuous monitoring of event logs, automated integrity checks, and reconciliation between physical timestamps and virtual twin states, ensuring consistent and trustworthy model behaviour.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real‑time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Bidirectional data exchange facilitated by the DT framework, supporting interoperability across heterogeneous systems.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle in this framework follows a model‑driven path that begins with BIM enrichment where Building Control Engineers annotate physical assets with explicit sensor, actuator, and controller semantics directly inside the BIM workspace. These enriched models are then subjected to model‑based engineering (MDE) transformations that map the BIM representation to a deployable semantic data model and automatically generate the corresponding DT artifacts (e.g., device configuration, connectivity adapters, and service descriptors). The conversion phase produces deployable artifacts that are staged for deployment to the runtime platform. Deployment, governed by the DT Engineer, respectively instantiates the digital twins and configures the real‑time data connectors so that every change in the physical building (via IoT gateways) is mirrored in the digital space. Throughout the operational phase, Facility Managers continuously monitor and interact with the twin through immersive visualization (Unreal Engine) and real‑time dashboards, while custom analytics plugins can be added at runtime via the framework’s standardized extension points. Lifecycle governance is explicit: Building Control Engineers own the modeling phase, DT Engineers oversee artifact generation and deployment, and Facility Managers drive operational interaction, ensuring traceability and a single source of truth.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering of the twin follows a model‑driven methodology that is fully automated: (1) device models, including sensors, actuators, and controllers, are defined inside the BIM environment to create a unified semantic foundation; (2) an MDE engine translates this enriched BIM model into an intermediate, platform‑agnostic representation, from which code generators produce deployable DT artifacts such as configuration files, runtime adapters, and plugin stubs; (3) the resulting artifacts are packaged and deployed by the DT Engineer onto an open‑source runtime stack (e.g., Eclipse Ditto or BaSyx) that exposes standard connectivity interfaces; (4) runtime monitoring is enabled by real‑time data exchange, while the framework’s modular architecture allows custom service plug‑ins and analytics modules to be added incrementally; (5) evolution is managed through standard deployment cycles where updated BIM annotations trigger re‑extraction and redeployment of the affected twin components, preserving backwards compatibility via the framework’s well‑defined extension points and documented API contracts.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

The description accurately identifies the primary users (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer) and the general service categories (real-time monitoring, optimization, control, visualization) which are all explicitly supported by the source text. The 'unified dashboard' description is also slightly more specific than the text's 'interactive visualization'. Supported by quotes: 'Building Control Engineer... Facility Manager... DT Engineer' (Table I), 'real-time monitoring', 'Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

The extraction is highly accurate and detailed, perfectly matching the source text. It correctly identifies the MDE framework, the role of BIM, the specific technologies used (Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine), the automation of runtime artifacts, and the supporting research projects (TwinLight and BOREALIS). It captures the technical depth regarding code generation, extension points, and real-time synchronization. block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the system's focus on real-time and continuous synchronization without inventing specific intervals not present in the text. The source explicitly mentions 'real-time data exchange' and 'live synchronization' using specific tools. Supported by quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'enabling live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins', 'continuous synchronization with physical assets'.

The source text describes a single model-driven framework and the roles associated with it (e.g., Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager). It does not mention multiple Digital Twin instances, hierarchical structures of twins, or coordination mechanisms between separate twins.

The description accurately synthesizes the system's orchestration, correctly identifying the service-oriented architecture, the 'central hub' role of the framework, and the specific user roles (e.g., 'Building Control Engineers... initiate DT deployment'). It captures the requirement for 'Runtime configuration management'. However, the score is reduced from 5 to 4 because the phrase 'Resource management is supported by a scalable broker' is not explicitly found in the text (the text mentions 'scalability' and 'Eclipse Hono' but not a 'scalable broker'), and 'common data models' is an inference rather than a direct quote.

The extraction accurately describes the framework's approach to external integration via standardized extension points and its emphasis on interoperability across heterogeneous systems. It correctly notes that specific protocols are not detailed in the text. Supported by quotes: 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
